sentences.text: Talking about.
And also the thing is that I want more details.
Because if I talk slow, I'll have to skip.
Yes, that's what I'm asking.
Yeah.
That's--
Yeah.
That's what I'm asking.
Because it's impossible.
Because what I said till now is I have to skip a lot of details because it's impossible.
Yeah, at that time the Russians started to attack the Germans.
They lost by then already Stalingrad, or what now it's named Volgograd.
They far away already, kicked them back from Moscow and from Leningrad.
It's now St.
Petersburg.
So they are starting to come already-- to kick them back.
Conscience?
Those people don't have a conscious.
They were just scared for their own skin.
They knew that if the Russians were coming, maybe once some Jews to survive.
They wanted to have somebody on their good side.
It's so cold.
They were nice people.
So they started to help.
So the children from, from Bershad, we had to be taken to Balta, to another ghetto.
Because the, the delegation from Romania to come to Balta to take the children, the orphans, to Romania, so--
It was about two days that we were taken by horse and carriage-- sleigh actually, it was winter-- about two days.
We were practical naked and after almost three years already.
So we were taken to Balta.
And when I arrived to Balta, nobody was there to pick us up.
We were put on all kinds of places-- old shacks, old rooms.
They said, we'll make lists for children that have to go to Romania.
So at last, the delegations came from Romania.
And they make the list of people, children, to go to a Romania.
One group left-- like me and my brother, we weren't on the, on the list.
We had to wait for the second, second, the group to go.
Meanwhile, the Germans came closer and closer.
The, the Balta, the ghetto of Balta was take over by the SS.
This is the real killers.
This is for them one thing, to kill Jews.
Even they could escape with the last [INAUDIBLE] army, but they decided to stay behind and kill Jews.
So happened that Balta was like in a valley and surrounded by mountains.
On the mountains were standing the Russians.
In the valley were the ghetto and the Germans, the SS [INAUDIBLE] to Jewish people, most of children.
So we were caught in the crossfire from heavy artillery, Russian and Germans.
And we are children in between.
If this wasn't enough, the Germans were running, killing, murdering, raping young girls.
This was six weeks.
In one room, we were 40 children, keeping each other-- we knew that we will be killed.
Suddenly, two Germans came in with the, with a German, German Shepherd.
And when they saw 40 children, they said, oh, we will throw a hand grenade.
And it will kill them all.
The reason they didn't throw any grenade, they didn't had one.
Because it was just before the Russians came in.
They were shooting and killing till the last bullet they had.
They even managed to hurt four-- about 50 people in a train.
And they burned them alive.
My brother was one of those.
And the last minute, he escaped.
It a miracle how we survived.
Just turned and he survived.
So in this room, when we were with those, with the Germans, was two young girls.
At that time, I didn't know what rape, what sex, I mean, was.
I was a young child.
And two girls-- one was 15, one was 16 years old.
And each time when the Germans came in, they used to lie down on the floor and take the younger children to sit on top of them.
I didn't know why.
And later, I find out they were afraid that they will be raped.
And this was for six weeks.
After those two Germans left our room, they came along the street.
There was a mother with a little girl, a little boy, a baby wrapped up.
They shot the mother of the child.
Blood was pouring on the streets.
Two days later, the Russians came.
All the streets were filled with blood of Jewish people and Jewish children, dead people.
They wouldn't allow to bury the people.
They made movies about this.
But does anybody see them?
We automatically became solid citizen.
We weren't children, Jewish children, survivors.
We were Soviet citizen.
They used us for propaganda to show they liberated Soviet citizen from the Nazis.
They was no mentioning we were Jewish children.
After-- and also, I forgot to mention, after when those two Germans left, we decided-- it might sound funny-- we are going to fight.
We are talking children.
How are we going to fight?
In the night, we are going to pick up some stones and keep the-- there was like a attic.
So the older boys was 15, 16 years old.
And the two girls will hide in the attic up there.
And now the smaller children will give them stones.
And when the German will come back, they will throw the stones from up there-- beautiful, beautiful fighting.
We were lucky they never came back.
Because if somebody come back and we throw stones, I wouldn't be here.
They would burn us alive.
Who knows what they would done to us?
Those Germans, they could run away.
They were taken prisoners by the Russians.
But for them to kill a little bit more Jews was more important than to save their own skin.
So when the Russians came, we were hungry with no food, no nothing.
We ask the Russian army to give us something to eat.
They said, don't worry.
Some day, you will have what to eat.
After three years, the Russians didn't have anything to give us.
We were afraid to stay behind in Balta because there were cases that the Russian-- Germans counterattack and the Russian gain into retreat.
And you can imagine if the Germans come back and find us all.
So we decided we have to go back to Bershad.
But there was a bridge.
It was river.
There was a bridge.
And the Germans destroyed the bridge, because they didn't want the Russians to cross the bridge.
But it was a wooden bridge, not like today from stones, from metal.
So we have-- wanted to go back.
There was the river.
So just the pieces of wood were floating on that river.
And we were jumping from one piece of wood to the other one.
It's a miracle that nobody drowned in that river.
And all the children, a few hundred children, were marching back.
I remember there was a little sister and brother.
He was, he was maybe 10 years old.
He was still sucking his thumb.
And his little sister, maybe five, six years old, the way he protected his little sister.
This boy was on the list to go to Romania.
But his little sister wasn't on the list.
And he didn't want to leave his little sister-- such a sacrifice.
Even on the next-- he might not have been at all on the list.
But to leave his little sister alone, and he gave away his place to another boy in my hometown.
And the boy said, see what I have, a warm hat.
The only thing what I can give you is the hat.
Keep the hat, and when they call your name, I will be going instead you.
This boy was in Israel.
He was in the Israeli army.
And he was twice wounded in the fights in [INAUDIBLE].
But this little sister and brother, he was holding her, wrapped in rags.
And those two children were marching back from Balta to Bershad to escape from those Nazis.
There was also a case, a young girl.
Her face was wrapped with rags.
She has a facial wound.
I haven't seen her for many years.
In '57, I was in Israel already.
I come into a lawyer's office because of the restitution money, had to be a witness of friend of mine.
Suddenly, I see a face.
This face became so familiar.
And I come to her and said, [?
Chana, ?] weren't you with the children marching back from Balta to Bershad?
And she looks at me.
She said, how do you remember?
I said, weren't you wrapped up in rags, your face?
She turned her face.
She had a big scar on her face.
It was the bullet wound.
The bullet was only taken out in Israel.
And I recognized her.
After so many years, I recognized this, this girl.
No, no.
No.
I mean this is, this is just children.
We were marching [INAUDIBLE].
Must have been a few hundred children.
Because one part left, one part we had to wait for the next time.
And meanwhile, the Russians came.
And, and that, that was the end of it.
Those children were taken to Romania.
Eventually, when the, when the Russians occupied Romania, those children were taken back to Russia, again for propaganda purposes.
They were taken to the-- in Bucharest at the train station with the Russian army making movies.
Those are our children, not Jewish children, our children.
Because the Romanian took them out.
And now we are taking them back home.
Instead to take them back home, the bigger children were taken in the Russia.
There was [INAUDIBLE].
At that time was named [?
Stalin, ?] to work in factories, and the smaller near Odessa in an orphanage.
Sisters and brothers were separated because their age were different age.
And those children even, who knows if they ever--
Those children separate because they, they couldn't, they weren't being together.
So everything was, was kept was just for propaganda purposes.
Here we were Romanian.
Here we were Russian.
Here we were-- to mention we are Jewish children, survivor of Transnistria, of the Holocaust, anything to mention that we are Jewish children.
It was never mentioned.
So when I came back to Bershad at last, I remember I was physically, emotionally so tired.
I didn't care anymore if I'm dead or alive.
I lied in that room where we were staying there.
I was lying down on a bench, so-called-- call it a bed, a bench.
I was lying down.
A piece of the wood from the ceiling fell maybe two inches from me.
If I lied down a little bit closer, that piece of wood would have killed me.
But I was lying down.
I didn't move.
I didn't care what's happening.
People in the room were, Freda, Freda, are you all right?
I, I just didn't care anymore.
I was exhausted physically and emotionally.
And after a few weeks, we had a rest.
We marched home.
And I mean marching home.
Because we were liberated in 1944.
The war only ended in 1945.
So all the transportation were kept for the army.
And the thing is all the Jewish men who survived this war already were taken in the Russian army to fight the Germans.
When I come Balta to Bershad, a lot of Jewish men were already gone in the Russian army.
Very few survived.
They fell.
They were killed in the fights because the wary ended in '45, a year later.
So this is what happened.
Yes.
It's very hard to know.
Could have been that I would have been somewhere in Russia.
Who knows?
Some Romanian Jews even adopted smaller children.
And the Russian took them out from their homes and send them back to Russia.
And everything was [? slowly ?] our people.
Our, our people, our children.
It would be somewhere mentioned, those are Jewish children.
Those were survivors.
Their parents died.
What were we doing in first place?
If we were Soviet citizen, what were we doing in the Ukraine in Transnistria?
Because we weren't there because we were Soviet citizen.
We were there because we were Jews.
And that what is painful-- because we were Jews.
And this is very important.
I'm talking about me personally.
I'm sure for generations to come, the people to know what was Transnistria?
What happened in Transnistria?
What happened to the Jews from Romania?
What were we doing there in first place?
Had we been only Soviet citizen, we wouldn't be there.
That is for me, from the future generations.
Because I was what, a child.
Fifty years later, I mean I don't know for long I will be living.
I mean, I was between the youngest survivors.

It's very important for the future generations to know what happened, that Transnistria should never be a forgotten cemetery, but to be known because Bessarabia, all province of Bessarabia, became like a ghost town.
Like from when I came home from Bessarabia, my hometown, over 5,000, 6,000 Jews before the war, only a few hundred survived.
I was the only child survivor, 12 years old.
Alone.
This, of this, we walked home.
Because all the transportation was kept for the military.
In Bershad?
Oh, just two or three weeks, just to have a rest because people started to go home.
So me and my brother we are alone.
We all saw people go, we go.
Because the people from my own hometown, from Ediniti.
So we're just going all together.
It's '44-- approximately April, April, '44.
That's right.
Exactly, because all of Europe was still occupied by the Germans.
Because we were on the Ukraine, Bessarabia [INAUDIBLE] was free.
Ukraine was free.
But all of Romania was still under German occupation.
Because remember, when we came home in '44, the city of [INAUDIBLE] which is on the border with the [INAUDIBLE] which is Bessarabia, was still under Romanian-- under German occupation.
So when we came home, I remember the Russians said, where are you going?
Do you know that all Romania is still under Germany?
But we couldn't take it anymore to be in those places, to be in Transnistria.
We want to go home, home no matter what.
But it was-- in a way, it was dangerous, too.
Because it's a war.
Nobody knows what from minute to minute happen.
Because actual the Allies, the D-Day started only June, '44.
And we were home already by then.
So it's-- I think the feeling home, to go home, it was unbelievable.
Unbelievable.
So no matter what, we just wanted to go home.
So we--
Sure.
We're marching home from town to town, from town to town.
When we came to Bessarabia, when we marched to Transnistria, the villager were throwing stones at us and screaming at us, and just how.
And when we came back, suddenly they became good.
I remember once, it was-- it must have been Easter.
Because you know, the Romanian Christian are Orthodox.
So they color their eggs.
So they brought us the Easter eggs.
But when we marched to Transnistria, nobody gave us anything.
But when we came back, unbelievable.
We were marching every day 35, 40 kilometer from point A to point B, sleeping cover overnight.
Yeah.
That's-- whereverit was place to sleepover, to have rest.
Now when people say, I have to go to the shopping, they you need the car?
We were walking 35, 30, 35 kilometer a day after almost three years of concentration camp, of Transnistria, of hunger, of starvation.
Many times I think, where did we get the energy?
Where did we get the strength to march back in a such a condition?
Me and my brother, a lot of people, I still were wrapped up in rugs because we didn't have any clothes.
So it was, when we arrived-- How long was the journey from [?
Brest ?] back to Ediniti?
Has to have been a few weeks.
I mean, I don't remember.
Who counted?
It just was such a time.
It's impossible.
Many times, I sit and talk to my husband, to my children.
And they ask me questions about those times, I--
So it's unbelievable.
It's unbelievable.
I mean, at that time, who would sit down and take notes?
Where the pencil?
What paper?
I mean, you go home.
So when we arrive to, to Bessarabia, town after town were ghost towns because all of Besarabia mostly were-- people died.
Or they were killed or died from hunger, starvation, cold.
Who could resist something like this?
I remember in one town we were passing until we came to Ediniti.
Only two ladies-- I don't know if they were sister-- two young girls, or they were sisters or just friends.
And they were going over the streets and screaming and tearing practically their hair out, said, nobody's left.
Nobody's left.
They were two people from whole town.
Nobody survived.
When I came back, Ediniti, as I said, it was 5,000, 6,000 Jews.
A few hundred survived.
I was only child.
I was alone.
My brother went back to Chernivtsi.
Because when we were living in the big city, my parents took some of our belongings to our next door neighbor.
And they said, look, maybe we'll return back.
Keep our belongings.
I remember carpets, like in every home.
So my brother went to Chernivtsi to pick up things from those neighbors.
She said nothing.
Everything was taken away.
And [INAUDIBLE] stole carpets, our curtains.
But what could he do?
He was 17 years old.
He couldn't do anything.
And he find some work.
Because my brother spoke very good Russian.
And it was, yeah, it was '44.
They send him to work in a place to teach how to defend against the poisonous gases.
I mean, at that time, now I don't know how it's now, but at that time, they were afraid if the enemy throws some poisonous gases.
So he became an instructor.
And he was working in another city.
And I--
Outside Chernivitsi, that's right.
No.
Only a few days.
And then he went to Chernivtsi, maybe to pick up a few of our belongings.
But then if he didn't pick up any things.
I've stayed in Edinitsi with this gentleman, [? so-called, ?] supposed to be our friend.
And he treated us-- because we survived.
So he treated me like-- it was-- I was like a maid, scrubbing floor, doing all manual jobs, in order to have a place where to live, a place where to stay.
I was all alone.
Our home was the KGB.
It was nationalized.
Everyone was afraid to look at [? them ?] because after all, my father was a rich man.
And I am-- and the Russian, I mean, I am a child of a, of a rich man.
This was a crime.
So-- and I was staying with this gentleman and also another couple.
Because most of the Jewish homes were destroyed.
So happened they-- the local people thought that the, the Jews buried their gold in the homes.
So destroying the homes, looking for gold.
So the few houses that were still left, people of few families or whatever people were living together.
So I was living with this gentleman-- I had my room-- and another couple which she lost her daughter in Transnistria.
This lady before the war was a little bit on her head.
And losing her beautiful daughter didn't help.
And she started to hate me.
Why her, her daughter [INAUDIBLE] is dead?
And why I am alive?
And she was so cruel to me.
If I cooked a little bit of food, we're cooking on the same stove, wooden stove with charcoal with ashes, she would put in ashes and charcoal in the food.
So and I was cooking for this gentleman.
And he thought that I did it on purpose.
And he used to beat me up brutally.
And I was afraid again to say what, that, what happens.
And he used to throw me out in the backyard in the night.
I was sleeping on the grass and crying myself, miserable, alone.
I didn't have water to bathe or to shower or to keep myself clean.
So I needed a comb.
I find a fork.
And the fork became my comb until I scratched my scalp.
And it became infected.
But I had nobody to talk to.
I went to visit my brother.
And this was the last time I saw him.
This was in '44.
And somehow, I had a feeling that I will never see him again.
And I had a chance to go back with a, with a military because the military man was from Ediniti.
And he took me back to Ediniti.
And I said goodbye to my brother.
And I was crying.
And he said, Freda, don't cry.
I will come.
And I will take you.
It won't take long.
But he couldn't come to take me because it's Russia.
It's not like here.
You want, you move here.
You went there.
He couldn't even come to visit me because at that time, in order to come from one town to another, you need a paper that you're allowed to travel.
As long-- nobody gave him-- there was no reason to let him go.
So I couldn't go there.
And he couldn't here.
And I haven't-- we were corresponding with each other.
But I've never, I've never seen him anymore.
Do you have any idea about what might have happened to him?
No.
No, nothing at all.
So when I came back to Ediniti, I decided I have to go to school.
What will happen to me?
So even I have no pencil, no paper, no books, I went to register myself to school.
So because I spoke Russian, they put me in grade four.
I'd-- I'd lost so many years, but because I spoke Russian.
The funny thing, they asked me math.
How did I know math?
They ask me how many kilograms there is in a ton.
I didn't know.
Now I know.
It's a thousand kilograms in a ton.
So that's why they put me-- I went to go in grade five because I spoke Russian.
They said, no, you don't know how many kilograms is in a ton.
So when I came back to the people who were living, they were laughing at me, said, how come you didn't know that a thousand kilogram is in a ton?
Sounds funny, but-- and I started to go to school.
How old were you?
12, 12 years old-- it was the year when I came back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I went to school.
I didn't have no pencil, no paper, and there weren't any books.
The Russian children, they had books.
Because they were-- the Russian children came with their families.
But I didn't had any books.
So at that time, Russian history and Russian geography, it's very difficult.
And to remember the names, the dates, the places, it goes back maybe 1,000 years ago.
And the teacher used to dictate notes.
So I used to sit and just look at the teacher's face, in the teacher's eyes, and memorizing every little detail.
I was so anxious to learn.
I still know Russian poems, Russian songs.
People come from Russia, they think I just an immigrant now also from Russia, because I remember all those things.
It meant a lot to me.
I wanted to learn.
And sometimes, I felt that I won't remember everything.
Near me was sitting a girl.
She didn't feel like taking notes.
So I said, know what?
I will take the notes.
And you give me the paper.
I will just look it [? off. ?] This how I learned.
And I was the top student.
From what, I don't know.
This-- then I was in grade five.
I needed more paper and pencils.
It was more difficult.
So this lady, when I was living with her, she was so mean at me, why I survived instead of her daughter.
And I came home.
I had to do my homework.
She wouldn't let me do my homework.
All-- every day, I came from work.

Is every day, I had to scrub the floors, bring water from the well, because here, [INAUDIBLE] the water-- it was a small town-- to carry the pails of water, scrub the floor, and doing the-- every, everything I had to do.
And she didn't give me anything to eat.
Sometimes she give me a piece of bread.
This was my life.
And I decided, what am I going to do?
I have to learn.
At last, I found out there is a Russian orphanage in a, in the forest near Ediniti.
Because in the Russian orphanage, they have children till grade four.
And at that time, I was in grade five.
They brought the children grade five and six to Ediniti, to the city, to go to the school.
And I met some children.
Those children didn't have anything what to eat either.
But at least they had some books.
They had papers.
They could learn.
So I went to the director of the orphanage.
I said, look, I need a place for to live.
I am alone.
I want to go to school.
She said, you know what?
If you bring for the board of education that you are a good student, I will take you into the orphanage.
If not, forget it.
You can live on the street, too.
So I was a good student.
They gave me a letter that I were a good student.
So they took me into the orphanage.
So this-- the orphanage was in the forest.
It for the-- for the holiday, for Christmas, New Year, we had to go to the orphanage.
So it was 12 kilometer, again to march.
I was practically still naked.
It was winter, snow, to stay until the orphanage over the New Year's holiday.
So at least they gave me a coat.
How did they gave a coat?
They took away from another child.
Because they wanted the people in town to see that the children from the orphanage are well-dressed.
But the children in the orphanage, they were hungry.
They were naked.
It was a mess.
So they took away a coat from another child to give to me that I am also dressed like all the children from the orphanage.
Freda, we'll cut it there.
Thank you.
Yeah.
Oh, I need a little water.
